Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

The last action was RESET which restored the board but didn't clear the game_over flag. Score: 0. No further actions will be taken per the explicit instruction to stop when game_over is detected.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to collect the yellow target, then check if the level completes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 79 actions; a strong score needs about 21 or fewer. You are 3.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The MOUSE action on the yellow target didn't seem to change anything - the board still looks the same. The player is still at the same position and the yellow target is still there. Let me check if the board actually changed and try ACTION7 instead.
Looking at the result: `board_changed: True` but the visual appears the same. Maybe the purple bar at the bottom changed. Let me check what ACTION7 does - it might be the "collect" or "interact" action.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model update:
- Game is active, step 80, level 1.
- MOUSE on yellow target didn't collect it (board_changed=True but likely just the timer bar).
- Player (blue) at (37-40, 50-51), yellow target at (38-39, 52) - adjacent but not collected.
- Need to try ACTION7 as the interaction/collect action.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try ACTION7 - it might be the "interact/collect" action
action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

# Check if yellow target is still there
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
yellow_exists = any(n['color'] == 'Y' for n in nodes)
print(f"Yellow target still exists: {yellow_exists}")

if not yellow_exists:
    print("TARGET COLLECTED!")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Yellow target still exists: True

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 failed with "Unknown action at index 1". The valid actions are LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7. But the error says "Unknown action at index 1" - that's odd. Maybe the action list indexing is 0-based and ACTION7 is at index 3? Let me check - valid_actions = ['LEFT', 'RIGHT', 'MOUSE', 'ACTION7'], so index 0=LEFT, 1=RIGHT, 2=MOUSE, 3=ACTION7.
Wait, the error says "Unknown action at index 1: 'ACTION7'" - this suggests that maybe the action system doesn't recognize ACTION7 as a valid action name. But it's in the valid_actions list...
Let me try just moving RIGHT again to see if the player can actually move and collect the target by overlapping with it. The player is at column 50-51 and the target is at column 52. Moving RIGHT should put the player at column 52-53, overlapping with the target.
Actually wait - let me look at the segmentation more carefully. The blue player has 6 pixels and bounds (37,50)-(40,51). The yellow target has 2 pixels at boundary [[38, 52], [39, 52]]. They're adjacent horizontally. If I move RIGHT, the player should shift right by some amount.
Let me try RIGHT again and check if the player actually moves and overlaps the target.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model update:
- ACTION7 failed (unknown action error despite being in valid_actions list).
- Player needs to physically overlap the yellow target to collect it.
- Let me try RIGHT to move into the target position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to move player into the yellow target
action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

# Check player and target positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        print(f"Yellow: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min = min(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min = min(p[1] for p in b)
        r_max = max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_max = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Blue: pixels={n['pixels']}, bounds=({r_min},{c_min})-({r_max},{c_max})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
